B1 Cell level systems.question: Define osmosis. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The net movement of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Name two structures found in plant cells but not in animal cells. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Cell wall (1 mark)
Chloroplast or permanent vacuole (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Explain how the shape of a red blood cell helps it carry out its function. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: It has a biconcave shape which gives it a large surface area (1 mark)
A large surface area increases the rate of oxygen diffusion into and out of the cell (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Describe three differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Eukaryotic cells have a nucleus whereas prokaryotic cells do not (1 mark)
Eukaryotic cells contain membrane-bound organelles whereas prokaryotic cells do not (1 mark)
Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller than eukaryotic cells (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: A student uses iodine solution to test for starch in potato cells seen under a microscope. Describe how the student should prepare and observe the slide. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Place a thin sample of potato tissue on a microscope slide and add a drop of iodine solution (1 mark)
Lower a coverslip carefully to avoid trapping air bubbles (1 mark)
Start with the low-power objective lens and focus before moving to a higher magnification if needed (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Explain why cells that are very small exchange substances more efficiently than cells that are very large. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Small cells have a larger surface area to volume ratio (1 mark)
This gives more membrane surface for diffusion relative to the amount of cytoplasm (1 mark)
The diffusion distance from the surface to the centre of the cell is shorter (1 mark)
Oxygen and nutrients enter quickly and waste products leave quickly enough for the cell's needs (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: A student places equal-sized potato cylinders into 0%, 5%, 10% and 15% salt solutions for one hour. Suggest two improvements to this osmosis method and explain why each would help. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Repeat each concentration several times (1 mark)
Repeats allow a mean to be calculated and make the results more reliable (1 mark)
Dry each potato cylinder in the same way before remeasuring mass (1 mark)
This removes excess surface solution so the final mass is more accurate (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Evaluate the use of stem cells in medicine. [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Stem cells can divide and differentiate into specialised cells (1 mark)
This means they could be used to replace damaged tissues such as nerve cells or bone marrow cells (1 mark)
Embryonic stem cells can form many cell types so they have high medical potential (1 mark)
There are ethical concerns about using embryos to obtain stem cells (1 mark)
There is also a risk of immune rejection or uncontrolled cell division if treatment is not carefully managed (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Plan an investigation to find the concentration of sugar solution that is isotonic with potato tissue. [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Prepare a range of sugar solutions of known concentration such as 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 mol per dm3 (1 mark)
Cut potato cylinders of equal length and diameter using the same cork borer (1 mark)
Measure and record the initial mass of each cylinder before placing one in each solution for the same length of time (1 mark)
Remove the cylinders, blot them dry and measure the final mass (1 mark)
Calculate percentage change in mass and plot a graph of percentage change against concentration (1 mark)
The isotonic concentration is where the line crosses zero percentage change because there is no net movement of water (1 mark)

B1 Cell level systems.question: Explain how the structure of the cell membrane makes it selectively permeable. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The membrane is a phospholipid bilayer (1 mark)
Its hydrophobic interior makes it difficult for many dissolved or charged substances to pass directly (1 mark)
Specific channel and carrier proteins allow only particular substances to move across it (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: State one function of amylase in digestion. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Amylase breaks down starch into maltose or smaller sugars (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Give two features of an alveolus that make gas exchange efficient. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: It has a very large surface area (1 mark)
It has a thin wall or a good blood supply or a moist surface (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Explain why arteries have thicker walls than veins. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Blood in arteries is under higher pressure as it leaves the heart (1 mark)
Thicker muscular and elastic walls help the arteries withstand and maintain this pressure (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Describe how bile helps digestion in the small intestine. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Bile is alkaline so it neutralises the hydrochloric acid from the stomach (1 mark)
This provides the optimum pH for enzymes in the small intestine (1 mark)
Bile also emulsifies fat into small droplets, increasing the surface area for lipase (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: A student mixes amylase, starch and buffer solution at different pH values and uses iodine every 30 seconds to see when starch has disappeared. Explain how pH affects amylase activity. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Each enzyme has an optimum pH (1 mark)
Away from the optimum pH the active site changes shape (1 mark)
This means fewer enzyme-substrate complexes form so the reaction rate decreases or the enzyme may be denatured at extreme pH (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Compare the structure and function of xylem and phloem. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Xylem transports water and mineral ions from roots to leaves (1 mark)
Phloem transports dissolved sugars such as sucrose around the plant (1 mark)
Xylem vessels are dead and hollow with lignified walls (1 mark)
Phloem tissue is made of living cells such as sieve tubes and companion cells (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Explain why mammals need a double circulatory system. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: One circuit carries blood from the heart to the lungs and back (1 mark)
The other circuit carries blood from the heart to the body and back (1 mark)
This keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood separate (1 mark)
It allows blood to be pumped to the body at high pressure, delivering oxygen and glucose rapidly to cells (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: A student investigates the effect of pH on amylase by timing how long it takes for starch to disappear at pH 4, 7 and 9. Evaluate this method and suggest improvements. [5 marks]
mark_scheme: A strength is that different pH values are compared using the same general method (1 mark)
A limitation is that only three pH values are tested so the optimum pH may not be identified precisely (1 mark)
Another limitation is that the student judges the iodine colour change by eye, which is subjective (1 mark)
Repeat each pH several times and calculate a mean (1 mark)
Use a water bath or keep enzyme and starch concentrations constant so pH is the only factor changing (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Explain how transpiration pull moves water through a plant. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Water evaporates from mesophyll cells and diffuses out through the stomata (1 mark)
This creates a tension or pull in the xylem (1 mark)
Cohesion between water molecules allows a continuous column of water to be pulled up from the roots (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Plan an investigation to measure the effect of light intensity on transpiration using a potometer. [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Set up a bubble potometer with a leafy shoot cut under water to prevent air entering the xylem (1 mark)
Place a lamp at measured distances from the shoot to change light intensity (1 mark)
Measure the distance moved by the air bubble in a fixed time or the time taken to move a fixed distance (1 mark)
Repeat each distance several times and calculate a mean rate of water uptake (1 mark)
Control variables should include temperature, air movement, humidity and leaf area (1 mark)
Plot mean transpiration rate against light intensity or lamp distance and use the trend to draw a conclusion (1 mark)

B2 Scaling up.question: Explain why villi are important in the small intestine. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Villi increase the surface area for absorption (1 mark)
This allows digested food molecules such as glucose and amino acids to enter the blood more quickly (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Write the word equation for photosynthesis. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Carbon dioxide + water -> glucose + oxygen (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: State two factors that can limit the rate of photosynthesis. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Light intensity (1 mark)
Carbon dioxide concentration or temperature (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Explain why temperature affects the rate of photosynthesis. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Photosynthesis is controlled by enzymes (1 mark)
As temperature rises the rate increases up to an optimum, but above the optimum enzymes denature and the rate falls (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Describe how to test a leaf for starch. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Boil the leaf in water to kill it (1 mark)
Heat the leaf in ethanol in a water bath to remove chlorophyll (1 mark)
Rinse the leaf and add iodine solution; a blue-black colour shows starch is present (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Compare aerobic respiration and anaerobic respiration in animals. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen whereas anaerobic respiration does not (1 mark)
Aerobic respiration releases much more energy than anaerobic respiration (1 mark)
Anaerobic respiration in animals produces lactic acid (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Explain how mineral ion deficiency can affect plant growth. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Nitrate ions are needed to make amino acids and proteins (1 mark)
A lack of nitrate causes stunted growth (1 mark)
Magnesium ions are needed to make chlorophyll (1 mark)
A lack of magnesium causes chlorosis or yellow leaves because less photosynthesis can take place (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: A pondweed is placed 10 cm from a lamp and produces 24 oxygen bubbles per minute. When the lamp is moved to 20 cm away it produces 11 bubbles per minute. Explain this change. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Moving the lamp further away reduces light intensity (1 mark)
Light intensity is a limiting factor for photosynthesis (1 mark)
With less light energy, less photosynthesis takes place (1 mark)
So less oxygen is produced and fewer bubbles are counted each minute (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: A student uses pondweed, sodium hydrogencarbonate solution and a lamp to investigate the effect of light intensity on photosynthesis. Evaluate this method and suggest improvements. [5 marks]
mark_scheme: A strength is that sodium hydrogencarbonate provides carbon dioxide so light intensity can be tested fairly (1 mark)
Counting bubbles is a simple way to estimate oxygen production (1 mark)
A limitation is that bubbles may differ in size so bubble counts are not very accurate (1 mark)
Measure the volume of oxygen collected instead of just counting bubbles (1 mark)
Repeat each distance and calculate a mean while controlling temperature with a water bath or heat filter (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Explain how the structure of a leaf is adapted for photosynthesis. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: The leaf is broad so it has a large surface area to absorb light (1 mark)
The upper epidermis is transparent so light can reach the palisade cells (1 mark)
Palisade cells contain many chloroplasts to absorb light energy (1 mark)
Stomata and air spaces allow carbon dioxide to diffuse through the leaf (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: Plan an investigation to find the effect of carbon dioxide concentration on the rate of photosynthesis in pondweed. [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Use different concentrations of sodium hydrogencarbonate solution to provide different carbon dioxide concentrations (1 mark)
Place the same type and length of pondweed into each solution (1 mark)
Keep light intensity constant by keeping the lamp at the same distance (1 mark)
Measure the rate of photosynthesis by counting bubbles per minute or collecting oxygen volume in a fixed time (1 mark)
Repeat each concentration several times and calculate a mean (1 mark)
Control temperature and pondweed size, then plot rate of photosynthesis against carbon dioxide concentration (1 mark)

B3 Organism level systems.question: State the role of chlorophyll in photosynthesis. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Chlorophyll absorbs light energy for photosynthesis (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Define habitat. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The place where an organism lives (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Give two abiotic factors that could affect the distribution of plants on a grassland. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Light intensity, temperature, soil pH, soil moisture or mineral content (any two, 1 mark each)

B4 Community level systems.question: Explain why random sampling is used when placing quadrats. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Random sampling reduces bias in where the sample is taken (1 mark)
This makes the sample more representative of the whole area (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Describe how energy is lost between trophic levels in a food chain. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy is lost as heat during respiration (1 mark)
Energy is lost in movement (1 mark)
Energy is lost in waste products or because not all parts of an organism are eaten or digested (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Explain how decomposers and detritivores help recycle materials in an ecosystem. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: They break down dead organisms and waste material (1 mark)
This releases mineral ions into the soil (1 mark)
Plants take up these mineral ions and use them to grow, so materials are recycled (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Compare using a quadrat with using a transect to study organisms in a habitat. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: A quadrat is used to sample organisms in a small, fixed area (1 mark)
A transect is used to study how distribution changes along a line or across an environmental gradient (1 mark)
Both methods can be used to estimate abundance or distribution (1 mark)
Both should be repeated to improve reliability or both require careful identification of organisms (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: A student places quadrats every 2 metres along a transect from a hedge into a field and counts the number of daisies in each quadrat. Explain how this method can show the effect of abiotic factors on distribution. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: The transect shows how daisy numbers change with distance from the hedge (1 mark)
Abiotic factors such as light intensity, soil moisture or wind exposure may change along this line (1 mark)
If the number of daisies changes in a pattern, this suggests distribution is affected by those changing factors (1 mark)
Measuring the abiotic factors at each quadrat allows the student to relate organism distribution to environmental conditions (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Evaluate the use of biological indicators to assess pollution in a river. [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Some organisms are sensitive to pollution whereas others are tolerant (1 mark)
A high number of sensitive species such as mayfly nymphs suggests the water is less polluted (1 mark)
A high number of tolerant species such as bloodworms suggests the water is more polluted (1 mark)
Using indicator species can be quicker and cheaper than carrying out many chemical tests (1 mark)
However, the results can be affected by factors other than pollution such as water flow, temperature or season (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Explain how eutrophication can reduce biodiversity in a pond. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Fertilisers washed into the pond add nitrates or phosphates (1 mark)
This causes rapid algal growth or an algal bloom (1 mark)
The algae block light so underwater plants die and decomposers increase (1 mark)
Respiration by decomposers reduces oxygen concentration, causing animals to die and biodiversity to fall (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: Plan a field investigation to compare species richness in a mown grass area and an unmown grass area. [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Use random sampling with quadrats in both the mown and unmown areas (1 mark)
Place the same size quadrat the same number of times in each area (1 mark)
Identify and record the number of different plant species in each quadrat (1 mark)
Calculate mean species richness for each area (1 mark)
Control factors such as time of year, quadrat size and sampling effort (1 mark)
Compare the mean values to decide which area has greater species richness (1 mark)

B4 Community level systems.question: State what is meant by a trophic level. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The position an organism occupies in a food chain (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Define gene. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A section of DNA that codes for a protein or characteristic (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: State the difference between genotype and phenotype. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Genotype is the combination of alleles an organism has (1 mark)
Phenotype is the observable characteristic produced by the genotype and environment (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: A pea plant has the genotype Tt, where T is the dominant allele for tall stems. State the phenotype of this plant and explain why. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: The phenotype is tall (1 mark)
The dominant allele T is expressed when it is present, so the recessive allele t is masked (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Describe how DNA controls the production of proteins. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The order of bases in DNA forms a genetic code (1 mark)
This code determines the order of amino acids in a protein (1 mark)
Different proteins produced lead to different structures or functions in cells (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: A man with genotype Bb and a woman with genotype bb have children. Use a genetic cross to determine the probability of a child having the recessive phenotype. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Correct parental cross shown as Bb x bb (1 mark)
Correct offspring genotypes shown as Bb, Bb, bb, bb or 1:1 Bb to bb (1 mark)
Probability of recessive phenotype is 1/2 or 50% (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Explain how natural selection can lead to evolution. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: There is variation within a population caused by different alleles or mutations (1 mark)
Individuals with advantageous characteristics are more likely to survive and reproduce (1 mark)
They pass on the advantageous alleles to their offspring (1 mark)
Over many generations the frequency of these alleles increases, changing the population (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Explain why bacteria can develop antibiotic resistance rapidly. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Mutations can produce resistant bacteria (1 mark)
Antibiotics kill non-resistant bacteria, leaving resistant ones to survive (1 mark)
Bacteria reproduce quickly by binary fission (1 mark)
The resistant allele spreads quickly through the population because resistant bacteria pass it on (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Evaluate the use of genetic engineering in medicine and agriculture. [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Genetic engineering can be used to make useful substances such as human insulin (1 mark)
It can also be used to produce crops with desirable traits such as pest resistance (1 mark)
This may improve yields or reduce pesticide use (1 mark)
There are concerns about long-term ecological effects such as genes spreading to wild plants (1 mark)
There are also ethical concerns about altering the genetic material of living organisms (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Plan an investigation using fast-growing plants to test whether light intensity affects plant height, showing how you would obtain valid results. [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Grow groups of the same species of plant under different light intensities (1 mark)
Use the same number of plants in each group (1 mark)
Measure plant height at regular intervals over a set time period (1 mark)
Control variables such as water supply, temperature, soil type and mineral supply (1 mark)
Repeat using several plants in each condition and calculate a mean height (1 mark)
Plot mean plant height against light intensity and use the graph to draw a conclusion (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: State one difference between a dominant allele and a recessive allele. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A dominant allele is expressed when only one copy is present, whereas a recessive allele is only expressed when two copies are present (1 mark)

B5 Genes, inheritance and selection.question: Explain how selective breeding can produce animals with desired characteristics. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Humans choose parents that show the desired characteristic (1 mark)
These parents are bred together and the offspring with the best features are chosen again (1 mark)
Repeating this over many generations increases the frequency of alleles for the desired characteristic (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Define pathogen. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A microorganism that causes disease (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Give two ways white blood cells defend the body against pathogens. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: They engulf pathogens by phagocytosis (1 mark)
They produce antibodies or antitoxins (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Explain how vaccination protects individuals and populations. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A vaccine contains dead or inactive pathogens or their antigens (1 mark)
This stimulates white blood cells to produce antibodies and memory cells (1 mark)
If many people are vaccinated the spread of the disease is reduced because fewer people can be infected (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Explain why antibiotics are not effective against viruses. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Antibiotics target structures or processes in bacteria such as cell walls or bacterial metabolism (1 mark)
Viruses reproduce inside host cells and do not have these bacterial structures, so antibiotics do not work on them (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: A student investigates the effect of antiseptic concentration on bacterial growth using agar plates and paper discs soaked in antiseptic. Describe a safe method for this investigation. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Spread a bacterial culture evenly across nutrient agar in a Petri dish (1 mark)
Place paper discs soaked in different antiseptic concentrations onto the agar (1 mark)
Seal the lid with tape and incubate at 25 degrees C, then measure the clear zone around each disc (1 mark)
Use aseptic technique and do not open the plates after incubation to reduce the risk of contamination or infection (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Explain how intensive farming can increase food production. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Animals can be kept indoors in warm conditions so they use less energy keeping warm (1 mark)
This means more of the food energy is converted into biomass for growth (1 mark)
Selective breeding, efficient feeding and disease control can also increase yield (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Evaluate the use of antibiotics in agriculture and medicine. [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Antibiotics can treat bacterial infections effectively in humans and animals (1 mark)
Their use can reduce illness and improve welfare or survival (1 mark)
Overuse creates a selection pressure favouring resistant bacteria (1 mark)
These resistant strains can spread, making infections harder to treat (1 mark)
Therefore antibiotics should be used carefully and only when necessary (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Explain why maintaining biodiversity is important. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Biodiversity helps keep ecosystems stable (1 mark)
Different species may provide sources of food, medicines or useful genes (1 mark)
High biodiversity makes ecosystems more resilient to environmental change (1 mark)
A loss of biodiversity can disrupt food webs and reduce ecosystem services humans rely on (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Plan a field investigation to compare biodiversity in two habitats using an index of diversity. [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Choose two habitats to compare and use the same sampling method in both (1 mark)
Use random quadrats or a transect with quadrats to record the number of each species present (1 mark)
Repeat the sampling many times in each habitat (1 mark)
Record the total number of organisms and the number of species in each habitat (1 mark)
Use the data to calculate an index of diversity for each habitat (1 mark)
Compare the values to decide which habitat has greater biodiversity (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: Explain how farmers can reduce the spread of plant diseases. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Remove and destroy infected plants (1 mark)
Use resistant crop varieties or crop rotation (1 mark)
Control insect vectors and clean equipment to prevent pathogens spreading between plants or fields (1 mark)

B6 Global challenges.question: State the role of antibodies in the immune response. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Antibodies bind to specific antigens on pathogens to help destroy them (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Define the term relative atomic mass. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The average mass of atoms of an element compared to carbon-12 / compared to 1/12 of a carbon-12 atom

C1 Particles.question: State the relative mass of a proton. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: 1 / approximately 1

C1 Particles.question: What is the charge on an electron? [1 mark]
mark_scheme: -1 / negative

C1 Particles.question: Name the subatomic particle with no charge. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Neutron

C1 Particles.question: Calculate the number of neutrons in an atom of chlorine-35. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Atomic number of chlorine = 17 (1 mark)
35 - 17 = 18 neutrons (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Explain why isotopes of the same element have similar chemical properties. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Isotopes have the same number of protons / electrons (1 mark)
Chemical properties are determined by the electron configuration / electrons in the outer shell (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: An atom of nitrogen-14 contains 7 protons and 7 neutrons. State the number of electrons in a neutral nitrogen atom and explain your answer. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: 7 electrons (1 mark)
In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons / atomic number (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Describe how the mass number and atomic number of an atom are related to its protons, neutrons and electrons. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Mass number = protons + neutrons (1 mark)
Atomic number = protons / number of protons only (1 mark)
Number of electrons in a neutral atom = atomic number / protons (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Explain why the relative atomic mass of chlorine is 35.5, not a whole number. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Chlorine has two naturally occurring isotopes (1 mark)
Chlorine-35 and chlorine-37 (1 mark)
The relative atomic mass is the weighted average of the mass numbers of all naturally occurring isotopes (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: An ion X²⁻ has 18 electrons and 17 neutrons. Identify the element X and determine its mass number. Explain your reasoning. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: If X²⁻ has 18 electrons, then neutral X has 16 electrons (1 mark)
Therefore, atomic number = 16, so X is sulfur (1 mark)
Mass number = protons + neutrons = 16 + 17 = 33 (1 mark)
The ion is sulfur-33 / ³³S²⁻ (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Define what is meant by an element. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance made of only one type of atom / atoms of the same atomic number

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: State what a compound is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance made of two or more elements chemically combined in fixed proportions

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Name an element that is a non-metal at room temperature. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Oxygen / nitrogen / chlorine / sulfur / carbon / phosphorus / iodine (or any other non-metal)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: State one property of ionic compounds. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: High melting point / conduct electricity when molten / conduct electricity when dissolved / brittle / soluble in water

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Explain the difference between a compound and a mixture. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A compound has elements chemically combined in fixed proportions while a mixture has substances physically combined in variable proportions (1 mark)
Compounds have fixed properties while mixtures can have variable properties / a new substance is formed in compounds but not in mixtures (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Describe how you could separate a mixture of salt and water using evaporation. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Heat the salt solution gently / warm (1 mark)
Water evaporates leaving salt crystals / the salt remains (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Explain why oxygen is classified as an element and carbon dioxide is classified as a compound. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Oxygen consists of only one type of atom / has only one atomic number (1 mark)
Carbon dioxide consists of two different elements (carbon and oxygen) chemically bonded together (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: State the steps used to separate a mixture of sand, salt and water. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Filter to separate sand from the salt solution (1 mark)
Evaporate to separate salt from the water (1 mark)
Alternative: add water to dissolve salt, filter to remove sand, evaporate to recover salt (1 mark for clear method)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Explain why covalent compounds generally have lower melting points than ionic compounds. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Covalent compounds are molecular with molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces (1 mark)
Ionic compounds have strong ionic bonds between ions (1 mark)
Less energy is therefore required to break the weak forces in covalent compounds than the strong forces in ionic compounds (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: A sample contains sodium chloride, calcium carbonate and water. Describe a method to separate all three substances. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Filter the mixture to separate calcium carbonate (solid) from the solution (1 mark)
Heat the filtrate / solution to evaporate the water (1 mark)
This leaves sodium chloride crystals (1 mark)
The calcium carbonate can be washed and dried / all three substances are now separated (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: State the definition of a chemical reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A process in which substances are changed into different substances with new properties

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Name one sign that a chemical reaction has occurred. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Temperature change / colour change / gas produced / precipitate formed

C3 Chemical reactions.question: What is meant by thermal decomposition? [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Breaking down a compound into simpler substances using heat

C3 Chemical reactions.question: State what law is represented by the equation: reactants = products. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Law of conservation of mass

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Write the word equation for the combustion of methane. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: methane + oxygen → (1 mark)
carbon dioxide + water (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Explain why mass is conserved in a chemical reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Atoms are not created or destroyed in a chemical reaction (1 mark)
The atoms are rearranged to form new substances / the same atoms that are present at the start must be present at the end (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Balance the following equation: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃ [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Identify the unbalanced atoms (1 mark)
4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: A student burns 2.0 g of carbon in 10 g of oxygen. The mass of carbon dioxide produced is 7.3 g. Use the law of conservation of mass to explain this result. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Total mass of reactants = 2.0 + 10 = 12.0 g (1 mark)
Not all oxygen reacted (1 mark)
Mass of unreacted oxygen = 12.0 - 7.3 = 4.7 g / more oxygen remained than needed (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Describe what happens when copper is heated strongly in air and explain the observations in terms of a chemical reaction. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The copper turns black / a solid black product forms (1 mark)
Copper reacts with oxygen in the air / copper + oxygen → copper oxide (1 mark)
This is a chemical reaction because new substances are formed with different properties (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: The thermal decomposition of limestone occurs according to this equation: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. Explain why this is a decomposition reaction and describe the practical procedures to detect the products. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: One compound (calcium carbonate) breaks down into two simpler substances (1 mark)
Heat is required to break the bonds / thermal decomposition means breaking down with heat (1 mark)
CO₂ is detected by passing gas through limewater which turns cloudy / milky (1 mark)
CaO remains as a white solid residue which when water is added reacts exothermically (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Define an oxidation reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A reaction in which a substance gains oxygen / loses electrons

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: State what type of reaction involves the formation of a precipitate. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Precipitation reaction

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Name the salt produced when hydrochloric acid reacts with sodium hydroxide. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Sodium chloride

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: In a neutralisation reaction, state what is produced in addition to a salt. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Water

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Write the word equation for the reaction between calcium and oxygen. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: calcium + oxygen → (1 mark)
calcium oxide (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Predict the products of the reaction between magnesium carbonate and sulfuric acid. Write the word equation. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: magnesium carbonate + sulfuric acid → (1 mark)
magnesium sulfate + water + carbon dioxide (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: A student adds solutions of sodium carbonate and calcium nitrate to observe a reaction. Describe what they would observe and write the word equation. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A white precipitate forms (1 mark)
sodium carbonate + calcium nitrate → (1 mark)
calcium carbonate + sodium nitrate (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Explain why copper does not react with dilute hydrochloric acid but zinc does. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Zinc is more reactive than copper / zinc is higher in the reactivity series (1 mark)
Zinc is more easily oxidised / loses electrons more readily (1 mark)
Copper is not reactive enough to displace hydrogen from the acid (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Write the word equation and symbol equation for the reaction between sodium and water. Identify the type of reaction. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: sodium + water → sodium hydroxide + hydrogen (1 mark)
2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂ (1 mark)
Oxidation reaction / displacement reaction / redox reaction (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: A metal X reacts with dilute sulfuric acid to produce hydrogen gas and a salt. A more reactive metal Y does not react with the salt solution of metal X. Explain what this tells you about the relative reactivity of metals X and Y, and predict what would happen if metal Y was added to a solution of metal X's salt. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Metal X is more reactive than metal Y / X is higher in the reactivity series (1 mark)
This is because Y cannot displace X from its salt solution (1 mark)
If Y is added to X's salt solution, no reaction would occur (1 mark)
Because X is more reactive than Y, so X cannot be displaced from its salt by Y (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Define the term rate of reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The speed at which reactants are converted into products / how much product is formed per unit time

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: State one factor that increases the rate of a reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Increasing temperature / increasing concentration / increasing pressure / using a catalyst / increasing surface area

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: What is meant by a catalyst? [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up / without being chemically changed

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Name one industrial process that uses a catalyst. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Haber process / contact process / cracking of hydrocarbons

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Explain why increasing the temperature of a reaction increases the rate of reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Particles move faster at higher temperatures (1 mark)
This causes more frequent collisions / collisions with greater energy, so more particles have sufficient energy to react (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Explain why crushing a solid reactant into powder increases the rate of reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Crushing increases the surface area of the solid (1 mark)
The increased surface area allows more collisions between reactant particles to occur per unit time (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Describe an experiment to investigate how the concentration of an acid affects the rate of reaction with a metal. Include how you would measure the rate of reaction. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Use the same mass/size of metal with hydrochloric acid of different concentrations (1 mark)
Keep temperature and volume constant (1 mark)
Measure the volume of hydrogen gas produced at regular time intervals / measure the time to produce a fixed volume of gas (1 mark)
Plot a graph of volume against time / compare the gradients of the curves (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: A reaction produces a gas. Describe how you could measure the average rate of reaction by collecting the gas. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Collect the gas in a gas syringe / measuring cylinder / graduated tube (1 mark)
Measure the total volume of gas produced (1 mark)
Record the time taken / calculate rate = volume ÷ time (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Two identical pieces of zinc are added to two beakers containing hydrochloric acid at different temperatures. Beaker A is at 25°C and Beaker B is at 50°C. Explain why the reaction will be faster in Beaker B and predict what other safety considerations would be important. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: At higher temperature particles have more kinetic energy and move faster (1 mark)
This leads to more frequent and energetic collisions (1 mark)
Safety: heat source control / eye protection / fume hood if toxic gases / management of exothermic reaction (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: An enzyme is used as a catalyst in an industrial process. Explain why enzymes are particularly useful catalysts and describe one limitation of using an enzyme compared to a traditional chemical catalyst. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Enzymes are specific and only catalyse reactions they are designed for / highly efficient (1 mark)
They work at lower temperatures than traditional catalysts (1 mark)
Limitation: enzymes are denatured at high temperatures (1 mark)
Or: enzymes are expensive / difficult to produce (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Name one fossil fuel. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Coal / oil / natural gas

C6 Global challenges.question: State one non-renewable energy resource. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Coal / oil / natural gas / uranium

C6 Global challenges.question: Write the word equation for the combustion of coal. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: coal (carbon) + oxygen → carbon dioxide

C6 Global challenges.question: Give one environmental problem caused by burning fossil fuels. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Produces carbon dioxide which causes global warming / increases greenhouse gases / produces sulfur dioxide which causes acid rain

C6 Global challenges.question: Explain why scientists are concerned about rising carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas (1 mark)
It traps heat which leads to global warming / climate change (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Describe how limestone can be used to reduce the effects of acid rain when sulfur dioxide is released into the atmosphere. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Limestone (calcium carbonate) is added to neutralise acidic gases / reacts with sulfur dioxide (1 mark)
The neutralisation reduces the acidity of the rain / prevents damage to ecosystems (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Explain why sustainable agriculture using renewable resources is preferable to relying on traditional extraction of fossil fuels. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Fossil fuels are non-renewable and will eventually run out (1 mark)
Burning fossil fuels produces greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change (1 mark)
Renewable resources can be replenished / sustainable methods do not deplete resources (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Describe the steps in the nitrogen cycle and explain why nitrogen compounds are important for agriculture. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Nitrogen from atmosphere is converted to nitrogen compounds by nitrogen-fixing bacteria / lightning (1 mark)
These are used by plants for protein synthesis and growth (1 mark)
Without sufficient nitrogen, crops cannot grow efficiently / nitrogen fertilisers are essential for food production (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: The Haber process converts nitrogen and hydrogen into ammonia under specific conditions. Explain why ammonia is important globally and describe the environmental challenges of producing a large-scale supply. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Ammonia is used to produce nitrogen fertilisers required for global food production (1 mark)
Without ammonia fertilisers, crop yields would be significantly reduced (1 mark)
Producing ammonia requires high temperature, high pressure and uses fossil fuels which produce carbon dioxide (1 mark)
Finding sustainable methods and renewable energy sources for the Haber process is a global challenge (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Evaluate the use of synthetic fertilisers compared to organic farming methods in terms of environmental impact, food production efficiency and sustainability. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Synthetic fertilisers increase crop yields efficiently / feed a growing population (1 mark)
However, excess fertilisers runoff into waterways causing eutrophication (1 mark)
Organic farming is more sustainable and avoids chemical pollution (1 mark)
But organic farming produces lower yields so requires more land / is less efficient to feed the global population (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: Explain what is meant by the term 'density' and describe how you would determine the density of an irregular solid object in the laboratory [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Density is mass per unit volume (1 mark)
Measure mass using a balance in grams or kilograms (1 mark)
Find volume by water displacement using a measuring cylinder/eureka can (1 mark)
Divide mass by volume or use formula ρ = m/V (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: A student observes that ice floats on water. Explain this observation in terms of particle arrangement and density [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Ice has a less dense structure than liquid water (1 mark)
Solid ice has particles arranged in a regular lattice with more space between them (1 mark)
Water has denser packing of particles so ice floats on top (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: Compare and contrast the properties of ionic compounds and covalent compounds. Explain these differences in terms of bonding and structure [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Ionic compounds have electrostatic forces between oppositely charged ions; covalent compounds have shared electrons (1 mark)
Ionic compounds typically have high melting points due to strong electrostatic attractions; many covalent compounds have low melting points (1 mark)
Ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved; covalent compounds typically do not conduct (1 mark)
Ionic compounds form crystals; molecular covalent compounds form discrete molecules (1 mark)
Covalent network structures like diamond have very high melting points due to continuous strong bonds throughout (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: State three physical properties of metals and explain each property in terms of their structure and bonding [6 marks]
mark_scheme: Good heat conductors - free electrons can move and transfer kinetic energy throughout the structure (1 mark)
Good electrical conductors - delocalized electrons are mobile and transfer charge (1 mark)
Malleable and ductile - layers of atoms can slide past each other without breaking metallic bonds as electrons adjust (1 mark)
High melting points - strong metallic bonding throughout the structure (1 mark)
Lustrous appearance - free electrons can absorb and re-emit light (1 mark)
Any 3 properly explained properties scores full marks (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: A piece of copper metal has a mass of 64 g and a volume of 7.14 cm³. Calculate its density [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use ρ = m/V = 64/7.14 (1 mark)
Density = 8.96 g/cm³ or 8960 kg/m³ (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: Explain why diamond is extremely hard while graphite is soft, despite both being forms of carbon [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Diamond has each carbon atom bonded to four other carbons in a continuous tetrahedral network (1 mark)
All bonds must be broken for the material to distort, requiring very high force (1 mark)
Graphite has carbon atoms bonded in sheets/layers with weak van der Waals forces between layers (1 mark)
Layers can slide over each other easily with minimal force required (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: A student measures the volume of a liquid using a measuring cylinder and finds it occupies 25 mL. The mass of the liquid is 35 g. Calculate the density in g/cm³ [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Density = mass/volume = 35 g / 25 cm³ or (35 g / 25 mL) (1 mark)
Density = 1.4 g/cm³ (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: Explain the difference between an element, a compound, and a mixture. Give one example of each [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Element: only one type of atom; example: oxygen gas/iron/gold (1 mark)
Compound: two or more elements chemically bonded in fixed ratio; example: water/salt/carbon dioxide (1 mark)
Mixture: two or more substances physically combined; example: seawater/sand and iron filings/air (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: Describe the structure of a simple molecular compound and explain why these compounds typically have low melting points [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Atoms within molecules are held by strong covalent bonds (1 mark)
Between molecules are weak van der Waals/intermolecular forces (1 mark)
Little energy needed to overcome weak intermolecular forces, so low melting points (1 mark)

P1 Matter.question: What is meant by relative atomic mass and relative formula mass? Calculate the relative formula mass of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃). [Ar: Ca = 40, C = 12, O = 16] [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Relative atomic mass is the average mass of atoms of an element compared to carbon-12 (1 mark)
Relative formula mass is the sum of relative atomic masses of all atoms in a compound (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: Define 'force' and state the SI unit. Name three effects that forces can have on objects [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Force is a push or pull (1 mark)
SI unit is newton (N) or kg·m/s² (1 mark)
Can change shape of an object (1 mark)
Can change direction of motion/velocity (1 mark)
Can change speed of an object (any three effects required) (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: A car of mass 1500 kg accelerates from rest to 20 m/s in 5 seconds. Calculate the magnitude of the net force acting on the car [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Calculate acceleration: a = v - u / t = 20 - 0 / 5 = 4 m/s² (1 mark)
Use F = ma (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: Explain what is meant by 'resultant force' and describe how to find the resultant when two forces act on an object in the same direction [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Resultant force is the single force that has the same effect as all forces combined (1 mark)
Forces in same direction add together algebraically/are summed (1 mark)
Resultant = Force 1 + Force 2 acting in same direction (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: A block of mass 5 kg is placed on a horizontal surface. A friction force of 12 N acts opposite to the direction of motion. What force is needed to keep the block moving at constant velocity? [2 marks]
mark_scheme: At constant velocity, acceleration is zero, so net force must be zero (1 mark)
Applied force must equal friction force = 12 N (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: State Newton's three laws of motion and give an example for each law [6 marks]
mark_scheme: First law: An object remains at rest or continues at constant velocity unless acted on by a net force; example: a book on a table stays at rest (1 mark)
Second law: F = ma or acceleration is proportional to net force and inversely proportional to mass; example: a heavier cart needs more force to accelerate (1 mark)
Third law: If A exerts a force on B, then B exerts an equal and opposite force on A; example: a person pushing off a wall moves away as wall pushes back with equal force (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward. At the highest point of its trajectory, explain why its velocity is zero but its acceleration is not zero [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Velocity is zero because the ball momentarily stops before falling back down (1 mark)
Gravitational force (weight) acts downward throughout the motion (1 mark)
This gravitational force produces constant downward acceleration of 9.8 m/s² even when velocity is zero (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: A friction force of 8 N opposes the motion of a toy car on a table. Calculate the coefficient of friction if the normal force is 40 N [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use F = μN, so μ = F/N (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: Explain using Newton's laws why a passenger in a car leans forward when the car brakes suddenly [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The car experiences a net force backward (friction/brakes) causing deceleration (1 mark)
The passenger's body tends to continue forward due to inertia (Newton's first law) (1 mark)
Friction force from seat is insufficient to decelerate the passenger at same rate, so body moves forward relative to car (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: A 2 kg mass is suspended by a string. Calculate the tension in the string when the mass is stationary [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Weight = mg = 2 × 10 = 20 N or 2 × 9.8 = 19.6 N (1 mark)
Tension = 20 N or 19.6 N (equal to weight at equilibrium) (1 mark)

P2 Forces.question: Describe how you would measure the coefficient of friction between a block and a surface using simple equipment. Include the forces you would measure [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Place block on surface and pull with a force meter until just sliding or at constant velocity (1 mark)
Measure the friction force from the force meter (1 mark)
Measure the normal force/weight using a force meter or calculate from mass (1 mark)
Calculate μ = Friction force / Normal force (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: Define 'electric current' and state the SI unit. Explain the difference between conventional current and electron flow [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Electric current is the flow of charge per unit time (1 mark)
SI unit is ampere (A) or coulombs per second (1 mark)
Conventional current flows from positive to negative terminal (1 mark)
Electron flow is from negative to positive terminal (opposite to conventional current) (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: Calculate the charge passed when a current of 2.5 A flows for 8 seconds [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use Q = It (1 mark)
Q = 2.5 × 8 = 20 C (coulombs) (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: Explain what is meant by 'potential difference' and describe how you would measure it using a voltmeter [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Potential difference is the energy transferred per unit charge (1 mark)
Voltmeter connected in parallel across the component (1 mark)
Measured in volts (V), with positive terminal to higher potential, negative to lower potential (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: A resistor has a resistance of 200 Ω and carries a current of 0.5 A. Calculate the potential difference across the resistor [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use V = IR (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: Explain Ohm's law and describe how to investigate the relationship between current and potential difference for a wire [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Ohm's law states that V = IR or current is proportional to potential difference at constant temperature (1 mark)
Set up circuit with power supply, ammeter in series, voltmeter in parallel across wire (1 mark)
Vary the potential difference using a variable power supply (1 mark)
Measure current and corresponding potential difference at each setting (1 mark)
Plot V against I; straight line through origin confirms Ohm's law if resistance is constant (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: Two resistors of 6 Ω and 4 Ω are connected in series across a 20 V supply. Calculate the total resistance and the current flowing through the circuit [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Total resistance = 6 + 4 = 10 Ω (1 mark)
Use I = V/R (1 mark)
Current = 20 / 10 = 2 A (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: State the differences between series and parallel circuits in terms of current, potential difference, and total resistance [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Series: current is the same throughout; potential difference divided between components; total resistance is sum of individual resistances (1 mark)
Parallel: current is divided between branches; potential difference is the same across each component; total resistance is less than smallest individual resistance (1 mark)
Or use 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 for parallel (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: A light bulb is rated at 60 W when operating at 230 V. Calculate the current through the bulb and its resistance [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Use P = IV, so I = P/V = 60/230 = 0.26 A (1 mark)
Use R = V/I = 230/0.26 = 885 Ω or use P = V²/R so R = V²/P = 230²/60 = 883 Ω (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: Explain why the resistance of a wire increases as its length increases and decreases as its cross-sectional area increases [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Longer wire: electrons collide with more ions/atoms, experiencing more resistance (1 mark)
Larger cross-sectional area: more charge carriers available per unit length, lower resistance per unit length (1 mark)
R = ρL/A where L is length, A is area, ρ is resistivity (1 mark)

P3 Electricity.question: A heating element draws a current of 8 A when connected to a 230 V supply. Calculate the energy dissipated in 2 hours [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Use P = VI = 230 × 8 = 1840 W (1 mark)
Convert time to seconds: 2 hours = 7200 s (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Define 'magnetic flux density' and state its SI unit. Describe the magnetic field around a bar magnet [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Magnetic flux density is the strength of the magnetic field (1 mark)
SI unit is tesla (T) (1 mark)
Strongest at the poles, field lines emerge from north pole and curve to south pole (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Name two methods of producing a magnetic field and describe one method in detail [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Method 1: Permanent magnet (1 mark)
Method 2: Electromagnet - A current-carrying coil wound around a soft iron core produces a magnetic field; field strength depends on current and number of turns (1 mark)
Method 3: Current-carrying conductor (accept any two with one detailed) (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Explain how an electromagnet works and describe how its magnetic field strength can be increased [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Electromagnet works by passing current through coiled wire wound around soft iron core (1 mark)
Current creates magnetic field around each turn of wire; field is strengthened by soft iron core (1 mark)
Field strength increased by: increasing current through coil, increasing number of turns, using iron with higher permeability (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Draw and label the magnetic field pattern around a straight current-carrying wire. Identify the direction of the field using the right-hand rule [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Field forms concentric circles around the wire (1 mark)
Direction determined by right-hand rule: thumb in direction of current, fingers curl in direction of field (1 mark)
Field strength decreases with distance from wire (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: A current-carrying conductor is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field. Explain what force acts on the conductor and how its direction is determined [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A Force (Lorentz force) acts on the conductor perpendicular to both current and field (1 mark)
Direction determined using Fleming's left-hand rule: thumb = Force, first finger = field, second finger = current (1 mark)
Force magnitude F = BIL where B is magnetic flux density, I is current, L is length (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: A wire of 0.5 m length carrying a current of 4 A is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.2 T. Calculate the force acting on the wire [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use F = BIL (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Describe the structure and operation of a simple electric motor. Explain how a commutator ensures continuous rotation [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Motor has rectangular coil rotating in magnetic field between permanent magnets (1 mark)
Current flows through coil creating forces on opposite sides in opposite directions (Fleming's left-hand rule) (1 mark)
These forces create a turning moment (torque) causing rotation (1 mark)
Commutator is split ring that reverses current direction every half rotation (1 mark)
This keeps forces in same direction relative to pole positions, maintaining continuous rotation (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Explain what a magnetic field is and how it can be represented using field lines [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Magnetic field is the region where magnetic forces can be detected on magnetic materials or moving charges (1 mark)
Represented by field lines drawn from north to south pole (1 mark)
Field lines never cross; density of lines indicates field strength; tangent to line shows field direction at that point (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: A solenoid consists of 200 turns of wire wound around a cylinder. When carrying a current of 0.5 A, explain what happens to the magnetic field strength if you double the current and double the number of turns [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Doubling current increases field strength by factor of 2 (1 mark)
Doubling turns increases field strength by factor of 2 (1 mark)
Combined effect: field strength increases by factor of 4 (1 mark)

P4 Magnetism and magnetic fields.question: Describe a method to determine the direction of a magnetic field using a compass at a specific point [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Place compass at the point (1 mark)
Compass needle aligns with field direction; north pole of compass points in direction of magnetic field (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: Define 'wave' and distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves. Give examples of each [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Wave is a disturbance that propagates through space or a medium transferring energy (1 mark)
Transverse wave: oscillations perpendicular to direction of energy transfer; example: electromagnetic waves or water waves (1 mark)
Longitudinal wave: oscillations parallel to direction of energy transfer; example: sound waves or seismic P-waves (1 mark)
At least one example of each required (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: State and explain the relationship between wave speed, frequency, and wavelength [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Wave speed (v) = frequency (f) × wavelength (λ), written v = fλ (1 mark)
For a given medium, if frequency increases, wavelength decreases proportionally (1 mark)
Speed depends only on the medium, not on frequency or wavelength (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: A sound wave has a frequency of 250 Hz and a wavelength of 1.4 m. Calculate the speed of sound [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use v = fλ (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: Describe an experiment to investigate the relationship between frequency and wavelength for sound waves in air [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Use a signal generator connected to a loudspeaker to produce sound at different frequencies (1 mark)
Use a tube with one closed end and measure the length at which resonance occurs (stationary wave formed) (1 mark)
At resonance, tube length = λ/4 for fundamental; calculate wavelength λ = 4L (1 mark)
Calculate speed v = fλ at each frequency; should remain constant (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: Explain the terms 'amplitude', 'frequency', 'wavelength', and 'period' using a wave diagram or description [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Amplitude: maximum displacement from equilibrium position (1 mark)
Frequency: number of complete waves per unit time in Hz (1 mark)
Wavelength: distance between adjacent crests or adjacent troughs (1 mark)
Period: time taken for one complete oscillation in seconds; T = 1/f (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: A loudspeaker is connected to a signal generator producing a frequency of 400 Hz. Calculate the wavelength of the sound wave if the speed of sound is 340 m/s [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use v = fλ, so λ = v/f (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: Describe what is meant by a 'stationary wave' and explain how stationary waves are formed [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Stationary wave is formed when two waves of equal amplitude and frequency traveling in opposite directions superpose (1 mark)
Results in pattern of nodes (no motion) and antinodes (maximum motion) (1 mark)
Nodes occur where waves always cancel; antinodes where waves always reinforce (1 mark)
Examples: plucked string, resonating tube, microwave in kitchen (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: Explain why electromagnetic waves do not require a medium to propagate while sound waves do [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Sound waves are mechanical waves; require particles to oscillate to transfer energy through medium (1 mark)
Electromagnetic waves are oscillations of electric and magnetic fields; fields can exist in vacuum (1 mark)
No medium required for electromagnetic radiation to travel through space (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: The fundamental frequency of a vibrating string is 120 Hz. Calculate: (a) the second harmonic frequency, and (b) explain why higher harmonics have shorter wavelengths [3 marks]
mark_scheme: (a) Second harmonic frequency = 2 × 120 = 240 Hz (1 mark)
(b) Higher harmonics have higher frequencies; since v = fλ and wave speed is constant, higher frequency means shorter wavelength (1 mark)
For same string length: f₁λ₁ = f₂λ₂ (1 mark)

P5 Waves in matter.question: Describe an experiment to measure the speed of sound in air using two loudspeakers or tuning forks and an oscilloscope [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Set up two loudspeakers emitting same frequency from a signal generator (1 mark)
Adjust spacing between speakers for constructive/destructive interference pattern (1 mark)
Measure distance between adjacent loud and quiet regions (1 mark)
This distance equals λ/2; calculate wavelength λ = 2 × distance (1 mark)
Calculate v = fλ where f is frequency from signal generator (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Define 'radioactivity' and explain why some isotopes are radioactive while others are stable [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Radioactivity is the spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable nuclei (1 mark)
Some nuclei have unstable nucleus-nucleon ratios (1 mark)
These unstable nuclei emit particles/radiation to achieve stability (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: State the three types of radiation emitted by radioactive materials and identify their relative ionizing power and penetrating power [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Alpha (α) particles: helium nuclei; highly ionizing, weak penetration (stopped by paper) (1 mark)
Beta (β) particles: electrons; moderately ionizing, moderate penetration (stopped by thin metal) (1 mark)
Gamma (γ) rays: electromagnetic radiation; weakly ionizing, strong penetration (requires thick lead/concrete) (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Write the nuclear equation for the alpha decay of uranium-238, showing the products formed [2 marks]
mark_scheme: ²³⁸U₉₂ → ²³⁴Th₉₀ + ⁴He₂ (1 mark)
Or: ²³⁸U₉₂ → ²³⁴Th₉₀ + ⁴α₂ (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Explain what is meant by 'half-life' and describe how to determine the half-life of a radioactive sample using a graph or data [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Half-life is the time taken for the number of radioactive atoms to reduce to half its original value (1 mark)
Plot activity/count rate against time on a graph (1 mark)
Find the initial count rate, then identify the time when count rate becomes half (1 mark)
This time interval is the half-life; repeat for subsequent halving periods to verify (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 8 days. If a sample initially contains 6400 atoms, how many atoms remain after 32 days? [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Number of half-lives = 32 / 8 = 4 (1 mark)
After 1 half-life: 3200 atoms; after 2: 1600; after 3: 800; after 4: 400 atoms (1 mark)
Or use N = N₀ × (½)ⁿ = 6400 × (½)⁴ = 400 atoms (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Explain the difference between irradiation and contamination in the context of radioactivity [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Irradiation: exposure to radiation from external source; radiation stops when source removed (1 mark)
Contamination: radioactive material enters body or gets on skin; continues to emit radiation (1 mark)
Contamination is generally more dangerous as cells are exposed continuously to internal radiation (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Write the nuclear equation for beta-minus decay of carbon-14 and identify the product nucleus [2 marks]
mark_scheme: ¹⁴C₆ → ¹⁴N₇ + ⁰e₋₋₁ + ν̄ₑ (antineutrino) (1 mark)
Or simplified: ¹⁴C₆ → ¹⁴N₇ + ⁰e₋₋₁ (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Describe a safe method to compare the activity of different radioactive sources in the laboratory [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Use a Geiger-Müller counter or gamma counter kept at constant distance from each source (1 mark)
Record count rate for each source over same time period (1 mark)
Subtract background radiation for each measurement (1 mark)
Compare net count rates; higher count rate indicates higher activity (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: Explain why radioactive materials should be stored in appropriate containers and handled with tongs or forceps [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Containers (lead-lined) reduce exposure to external radiation from the source (1 mark)
Tongs or forceps keep distance between hands and source, reducing exposure (1 mark)
Prevents contamination of hands and ingestion/inhalation of radioactive material (1 mark)

P6 Radioactivity.question: A sample of iodine-131 (half-life 8 days) is used in medical treatment. What fraction of the original sample remains after 24 days? [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Number of half-lives = 24 / 8 = 3 (1 mark)
Fraction remaining = (½)³ = 1/8 or 0.125 (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: Define 'energy' and state its SI unit. Explain the principle of conservation of energy [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy is the capacity to do work (1 mark)
SI unit is joule (J) (1 mark)
Total energy in a closed system remains constant; energy can transform between forms but is not created or destroyed (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: Distinguish between 'work' and 'energy'. Calculate the work done when a force of 50 N moves an object 2 m in the direction of the force [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Work is energy transferred when a force causes displacement in direction of force (1 mark)
Energy is capacity to do work or cause change (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: Explain what is meant by 'power' and state the SI unit. Calculate the power required to do 600 J of work in 15 seconds [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Power is the rate of energy transfer or work done per unit time (1 mark)
SI unit is watt (W) or joule per second (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: A ball of mass 2 kg is dropped from a height of 5 m. Calculate: (a) its potential energy at the top, and (b) its kinetic energy just before hitting the ground, ignoring air resistance [4 marks]
mark_scheme: (a) Ep = mgh = 2 × 10 × 5 = 100 J (or use g = 9.8 to get 98 J) (1 mark)
(b) By conservation of energy, Ek = Ep = 100 J (1 mark)
Alternatively: calculate v from v² = u² + 2gh; then Ek = ½mv² (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: Describe the energy transformations that occur when a ball is thrown vertically upward and returns to the same height [4 marks]
mark_scheme: At release: kinetic energy maximum, potential energy minimum (1 mark)
Rising: kinetic energy decreases, potential energy increases (1 mark)
At highest point: kinetic energy zero, potential energy maximum (1 mark)
Falling: potential energy decreases, kinetic energy increases (returns to initial state ignoring air resistance) (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: A motor with power rating 500 W operates for 2 hours. Calculate the energy consumed in joules and kilowatt-hours [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Time = 2 hours = 7200 seconds (1 mark)
E = Pt = 0.5 kW × 2 h = 1 kWh (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: Explain what is meant by 'efficiency' and describe how to calculate the efficiency of an appliance or system [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Efficiency is the fraction of input energy converted to useful output energy (1 mark)
Calculated as: Efficiency = Useful energy output / Total energy input (1 mark)
Often expressed as percentage: (Useful output / Input) × 100% (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: A cyclist accelerates from rest to 8 m/s over 10 seconds. If the cyclist's mass (including bike) is 80 kg, calculate the kinetic energy gained [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Use Ek = ½mv² (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: Describe a method to measure the efficiency of an electric kettle by measuring specific energy input and energy used to heat water [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Measure mass of water using balance (1 mark)
Note initial temperature of water using thermometer (1 mark)
Record electrical energy input from meter or calculate from P×t (1 mark)
Measure final temperature; use E = mcΔT to find energy used to heat water (1 mark)
Efficiency = Energy used to heat / Electrical energy input × 100% (1 mark)

P7 Energy.question: A light bulb converts 5% of electrical energy to light and 95% to heat. If the bulb is rated 60 W, calculate the power wasted as heat [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Power wasted as heat = 95% of 60 W (1 mark)
Power wasted = 0.95 × 60 = 57 W (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Define 'renewable' and 'non-renewable' energy sources. Give two examples of each [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Renewable: energy from natural sources continuously replenished; examples: solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal (any 2) (1 mark)
Non-renewable: energy from finite sources being depleted; examples: fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas), nuclear (uranium) (any 2) (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Explain how a coal-powered power station converts chemical energy into electrical energy. State the main energy transformation steps [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Coal burned in furnace releasing chemical energy as heat (1 mark)
Heat boils water to produce steam under pressure (1 mark)
Steam forces turbines to rotate, converting heat energy to kinetic energy (1 mark)
Turbines connected to generator converting mechanical energy to electrical energy (1 mark)
Electrical energy transmitted via grid to consumers (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Describe how a wind turbine generates electricity and explain one advantage and one disadvantage of wind power [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Wind forces rotor blades to spin; mechanical energy drives generator to produce electricity (1 mark)
Advantage: renewable, no emissions, low operating costs; or one specific advantage (1 mark)
Disadvantage: intermittent/weather dependent, noise, visual impact, or affects wildlife; or one specific disadvantage (1 mark)
A complete answer must include one explained advantage and one explained disadvantage of wind power (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Explain the greenhouse effect and state how increased atmospheric CO₂ from burning fossil fuels contributes to global warming [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Greenhouse gases (CO₂, methane, water vapor) absorb infrared radiation from Earth's surface (1 mark)
Normally some heat escapes; increased greenhouse gases trap more heat (1 mark)
Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ into atmosphere, increasing concentration (1 mark)
Enhanced greenhouse effect causes global temperature to rise leading to climate change (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Compare the advantages and disadvantages of using nuclear power versus fossil fuel power for electricity generation [5 marks]
mark_scheme: Nuclear advantages: no CO₂ emissions, high energy density, high power output; disadvantages: radioactive waste disposal, safety concerns, decommissioning costs (1 mark)
Fossil fuel advantages: established technology, reliable baseload power; disadvantages: CO₂ emissions cause climate change, air pollution, finite resources (1 mark)
Nuclear and fossil fuels both have high initial costs but different environmental impacts (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: A solar panel has an efficiency of 15%. If solar radiation provides 1000 W/m² on a 2 m² panel, calculate the electrical power output [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Input power = 1000 × 2 = 2000 W (1 mark)
Output = 15% × 2000 = 300 W (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Explain how fossil fuel combustion relates to air pollution and describe two pollutants produced and their effects [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Fossil fuels contain carbon, hydrogen, sulfur compounds; combustion produces pollutants (1 mark)
Nitrogen oxides (NOₓ) formed at high temperatures; cause photochemical smog and acid rain (1 mark)
Sulfur dioxide (SO₂) from sulfur in fuel; causes acid rain and respiratory problems (1 mark)
Carbon monoxide (CO) from incomplete combustion; toxic, reduces oxygen carrying capacity of blood (1 mark)
Any two pollutants with effects required (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Describe how a hydroelectric power station generates electricity and identify one environmental concern associated with dam construction [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Water held in reservoir behind dam; gravity pulls water downward through pipes (1 mark)
Falling water spins turbines connected to generators producing electricity (1 mark)
Environmental concern: disruption to ecosystems, habitat loss, river flow changes, methane from reservoir, or displacement of populations (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: Explain why developing countries face challenges in transitioning from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources [3 marks]
mark_scheme: High upfront capital costs for renewable infrastructure (solar panels, wind turbines) (1 mark)
Existing fossil fuel infrastructure already in place and cheaper short-term (1 mark)
Energy demand growing rapidly; renewable sources may not meet immediate needs without storage (1 mark)

P8 Global challenges.question: A wind farm with 50 turbines operates at average power of 2 MW per turbine for 24 hours. Calculate the total electrical energy generated and determine the number of homes this supplies if each home uses 18 kWh per day [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Total power = 50 × 2 MW = 100 MW (1 mark)
Energy in 24 hours = 100 MW × 24 h = 2400 MWh = 2.4 GWh (1 mark)
Number of homes = 2400 MWh / 18 kWh = 133,333 homes (approximately 133,000) (1 mark)
